December 30, 1979(1979-12-30) (aged 77)  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S.
[Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") ([BA](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts")) [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School")

(June 28, 1902 – December 30, 1979) was an American [composer](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") who worked primarily in [musical theater](/wiki/Musical_theater "Musical theater"). With 43 [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") musicals and over 900 songs to his credit, Rodgers was one of the best-known American composers of the 20th century, and his work significantly influenced popular music.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American composer (1902–1979)

For other people named Richard Rodgers, see [Richard Rodgers (disambiguation)](/wiki/Richard_Rodgers_(disambiguation) "Richard Rodgers (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Richard Rogers](/wiki/Richard_Rogers "Richard Rogers") or [Dick Rogers](/wiki/Dick_Rogers "Dick Rogers").

Musical artist

**Richard Charles Rodgers** (June 28, 1902 – December 30, 1979) was an American [composer](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") who worked primarily in [musical theater](/wiki/Musical_theater "Musical theater"). With 43 [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") musicals and over 900 songs to his credit, Rodgers was one of the best-known American composers of the 20th century, and his work significantly influenced popular music.

Rodgers is known for his songwriting partnerships, first with lyricist [Lorenz Hart](/wiki/Lorenz_Hart "Lorenz Hart") and then with [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II"). With Hart he wrote musicals throughout the 1920s and 1930s, including *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(musical) "Pal Joey (musical)")*, *[A Connecticut Yankee](/wiki/A_Connecticut_Yankee_(musical) "A Connecticut Yankee (musical)")*, *[On Your Toes](/wiki/On_Your_Toes "On Your Toes")*, and *[Babes in Arms](/wiki/Babes_in_Arms "Babes in Arms").* With Hammerstein he wrote musicals in the 1940s and 1950s, such as *[Oklahoma!](/wiki/Oklahoma! "Oklahoma!")*, *[Flower Drum Song](/wiki/Flower_Drum_Song "Flower Drum Song")*, *[Carousel](/wiki/Carousel_(musical) "Carousel (musical)")*, *[Cinderella](/wiki/Cinderella "Cinderella")*, *[South Pacific](/wiki/South_Pacific_(musical) "South Pacific (musical)")*, *[The King and I](/wiki/The_King_and_I "The King and I")*, and *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music "The Sound of Music")*. His collaborations with Hammerstein, in particular, are celebrated for bringing the Broadway musical to new maturity by telling stories that focus on characters and drama rather than the genre's earlier light-hearted entertainment.

Rodgers was the first person to win all four of the top American entertainment awards in theater, film, recording, and television—an [Emmy](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards"), a [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"), an [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), and a [Tony](/wiki/Tony_Awards "Tony Awards")—now known collectively as an [EGOT](/wiki/EGOT "EGOT").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He also won a [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize"), making him the first person to receive all five awards.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 1978, Rodgers was in the inaugural group of [Kennedy Center Honorees](/wiki/The_Kennedy_Center_Honors "The Kennedy Center Honors") for lifetime achievement in the arts.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Fly_With_Me_poster.jpg/250px-Fly_With_Me_poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fly_With_Me_poster.jpg)

The poster for *[Fly With Me](/wiki/Fly_With_Me_(musical) "Fly With Me (musical)")*, the 1920 Columbia University [Varsity Show](/wiki/Varsity_Show "Varsity Show"). The music was co-written by Rodgers and [Lorenz Hart](/wiki/Lorenz_Hart "Lorenz Hart"), and also included songs by [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II"), making the show one of the first collaborations between the two men.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

Rodgers was born into a [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") family in Queens, New York, the son of William Abrahams Rodgers, a prominent physician who had changed the family name from Rogazinsky, and Mamie Rodgers ([née](/wiki/Birth_name#Maiden_and_married_names "Birth name") Levy). He began playing the piano at age six. He attended P.S. 166, [Townsend Harris Hall](/wiki/Townsend_Harris_Hall_Prep_School "Townsend Harris Hall Prep School"), and [DeWitt Clinton High School](/wiki/DeWitt_Clinton_High_School "DeWitt Clinton High School"). Rodgers spent his early teenage summers at Camp Wigwam in [Waterford, Maine](/wiki/Waterford,_Maine "Waterford, Maine"), where he composed some of his first songs.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Rodgers, [Lorenz Hart](/wiki/Lorenz_Hart "Lorenz Hart"), and [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II") all attended [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"). At Columbia, Rodgers joined the [Pi Lambda Phi](/wiki/Pi_Lambda_Phi "Pi Lambda Phi") fraternity. In 1921, he began studies at the Institute of Musical Art (now the [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School")).[[6]](#cite_note-6) Rodgers was influenced by composers such as [Victor Herbert](/wiki/Victor_Herbert "Victor Herbert") and [Jerome Kern](/wiki/Jerome_Kern "Jerome Kern"), as well as by the [operettas](/wiki/Operetta "Operetta") his parents took him to see on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") when he was a child.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Rodgers and Hart

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Rodgers and Hart")]

Main article: [Rodgers and Hart](/wiki/Rodgers_and_Hart "Rodgers and Hart")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Rodgers_and_Hart_NYWTS.jpg/250px-Rodgers_and_Hart_NYWTS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rodgers_and_Hart_NYWTS.jpg)

Richard Rodgers (seated) with [Lorenz Hart](/wiki/Lorenz_Hart "Lorenz Hart") in 1936

In 1919, Rodgers met [Lorenz Hart](/wiki/Lorenz_Hart "Lorenz Hart"), thanks to Phillip Levitt, a friend of Rodgers's older brother. [Rodgers and Hart](/wiki/Rodgers_and_Hart "Rodgers and Hart") struggled for years in the field of musical comedy, writing several amateur shows. They made their professional debut with the song "Any Old Place With You", featured in the 1919 Broadway musical comedy *A Lonely Romeo*. Their first professional production was *[Poor Little Ritz Girl](/wiki/Poor_Little_Ritz_Girl "Poor Little Ritz Girl")* (1920), which also had music by [Sigmund Romberg](/wiki/Sigmund_Romberg "Sigmund Romberg"). Their next professional show, *The Melody Man*, did not premiere until 1924.

When he was just out of college Rodgers worked as musical director for [Lew Fields](/wiki/Lew_Fields "Lew Fields"). Among the stars he accompanied were [Nora Bayes](/wiki/Nora_Bayes "Nora Bayes") and [Fred Allen](/wiki/Fred_Allen "Fred Allen").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Rodgers was considering quitting show business altogether to sell children's underwear when he and Hart finally broke through in 1925. They wrote the songs for a benefit show presented by the prestigious [Theatre Guild](/wiki/Theatre_Guild "Theatre Guild") called *[The Garrick Gaieties](/wiki/The_Garrick_Gaieties "The Garrick Gaieties")*, and critics found the show fresh and delightful. Although it was meant to run only one day, the Guild knew it had a success and allowed it to reopen later. The show's biggest hit—the song that Rodgers believed "made" Rodgers and Hart—was "[Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_(song) "Manhattan (song)")". The two were now a Broadway songwriting force.

During the rest of the decade, the duo wrote several hit shows for both Broadway and London, including *[Dearest Enemy](/wiki/Dearest_Enemy "Dearest Enemy")* (1925), *[The Girl Friend](/wiki/The_Girl_Friend "The Girl Friend")* (1926), *[Peggy-Ann](/wiki/Peggy-Ann "Peggy-Ann")* (1926), *[A Connecticut Yankee](/wiki/A_Connecticut_Yankee_(musical) "A Connecticut Yankee (musical)")* (1927), and *[Present Arms](/wiki/Present_Arms_(musical) "Present Arms (musical)")* (1928). Their 1920s shows produced standards such as "[Here in My Arms](/wiki/Here_in_My_Arms "Here in My Arms")", "[Mountain Greenery](/wiki/Mountain_Greenery "Mountain Greenery")", "[Blue Room](/wiki/Blue_Room_(1926_song) "Blue Room (1926 song)")", "[My Heart Stood Still](/wiki/My_Heart_Stood_Still "My Heart Stood Still")", and "[You Took Advantage of Me](/wiki/You_Took_Advantage_of_Me "You Took Advantage of Me")".

With the [Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") in full swing during the first half of the 1930s, the team sought greener pastures in Hollywood. The hardworking Rodgers later regretted these relatively fallow years, but he and Hart wrote some classic songs and film scores out west, including *[Love Me Tonight](/wiki/Love_Me_Tonight "Love Me Tonight")* (1932) (directed by [Rouben Mamoulian](/wiki/Rouben_Mamoulian "Rouben Mamoulian"), who later directed Rodgers's *[Oklahoma!](/wiki/Oklahoma! "Oklahoma!")* on Broadway), which introduced three standards: "[Lover](/wiki/Lover_(Rodgers_and_Hart_song) "Lover (Rodgers and Hart song)")", "[Mimi](/wiki/Mimi_(song) "Mimi (song)")", and "[Isn't It Romantic?](/wiki/Isn%27t_It_Romantic%3F "Isn't It Romantic?")". Rodgers also wrote a melody for which Hart wrote three consecutive lyrics that were either cut, not recorded or not a hit. The fourth lyric resulted in one of their most famous songs, "[Blue Moon](/wiki/Blue_Moon_(1934_song) "Blue Moon (1934 song)")". Other film work includes the scores to *[The Phantom President](/wiki/The_Phantom_President "The Phantom President")* (1932), starring [George M. Cohan](/wiki/George_M._Cohan "George M. Cohan"), *[Hallelujah, I'm a Bum](/wiki/Hallelujah,_I%27m_a_Bum_(film) "Hallelujah, I'm a Bum (film)")* (1933), starring [Al Jolson](/wiki/Al_Jolson "Al Jolson"), and, in a quick return after having left Hollywood, *Mississippi* (1935), starring [Bing Crosby](/wiki/Bing_Crosby "Bing Crosby") and [W. C. Fields](/wiki/W._C._Fields "W. C. Fields").

In 1935, they returned to Broadway and wrote an almost unbroken string of hit shows that ended shortly before Hart's death in 1943. Among the most notable are *[Jumbo](/wiki/Jumbo_(musical) "Jumbo (musical)")* (1935), *[On Your Toes](/wiki/On_Your_Toes "On Your Toes")* (1936, which included the ballet "[Slaughter on Tenth Avenue](/wiki/Slaughter_on_Tenth_Avenue "Slaughter on Tenth Avenue")", choreographed by George Balanchine), *[Babes in Arms](/wiki/Babes_in_Arms "Babes in Arms")* (1937), *[I Married an Angel](/wiki/I_Married_an_Angel "I Married an Angel")* (1938), *[The Boys from Syracuse](/wiki/The_Boys_from_Syracuse "The Boys from Syracuse")* (1938), *[Pal Joey](/wiki/Pal_Joey_(musical) "Pal Joey (musical)")* (1940), and their last original work, *[By Jupiter](/wiki/By_Jupiter "By Jupiter")* (1942). Rodgers also contributed to the book on several of these shows.

Many of the songs from these shows are still sung and remembered, including "[The Most Beautiful Girl in the World](/wiki/The_Most_Beautiful_Girl_in_the_World_(1935_song) "The Most Beautiful Girl in the World (1935 song)")", "[My Romance](/wiki/My_Romance_(song) "My Romance (song)")", "[Little Girl Blue](/wiki/Little_Girl_Blue_(song) "Little Girl Blue (song)")", "[I'll Tell the Man in the Street](/wiki/I%27ll_Tell_the_Man_in_the_Street "I'll Tell the Man in the Street")", "[There's a Small Hotel](/wiki/There%27s_a_Small_Hotel "There's a Small Hotel")", "[Where or When](/wiki/Where_or_When "Where or When")", "[My Funny Valentine](/wiki/My_Funny_Valentine "My Funny Valentine")", "[The Lady Is a Tramp](/wiki/The_Lady_Is_a_Tramp "The Lady Is a Tramp")", "[Falling in Love with Love](/wiki/Falling_in_Love_with_Love "Falling in Love with Love")", "[Bewitched, Bothered and Bewildered](/wiki/Bewitched,_Bothered_and_Bewildered "Bewitched, Bothered and Bewildered")", and "[Wait till You See Her](/wiki/Wait_till_You_See_Her "Wait till You See Her")".

In 1939, Rodgers wrote the ballet *Ghost Town* for the [Ballet Russe de Monte Carlo](/wiki/Ballet_Russe_de_Monte_Carlo "Ballet Russe de Monte Carlo"), with choreography by [Marc Platoff](/wiki/Marc_Platt_(dancer) "Marc Platt (dancer)").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Rodgers and Hammerstein

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Rodgers and Hammerstein")]

Main article: [Rodgers and Hammerstein](/wiki/Rodgers_and_Hammerstein "Rodgers and Hammerstein")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Rodgers_and_Hammerstein_at_piano-original.jpg/250px-Rodgers_and_Hammerstein_at_piano-original.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rodgers_and_Hammerstein_at_piano-original.jpg)

Rodgers (seated) with Hammerstein, 1945

Rodgers's partnership with Hart began to flounder because of Hart's unreliability and declining health from [alcoholism](/wiki/Alcoholism "Alcoholism"). Rodgers began working again with [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II"), with whom he had written songs before ever working with Hart. Their first musical, the groundbreaking hit *[Oklahoma!](/wiki/Oklahoma! "Oklahoma!")* (1943), is a notable example of a "[book musical](/wiki/Book_musical "Book musical")", a musical play in which the songs and dances are fully integrated into the plot. What was once a collection of songs, dances, and comic turns held together by a tenuous plot became a fully integrated narrative. *[Show Boat](/wiki/Show_Boat "Show Boat")* is considered the earliest example of a book musical, but *Oklahoma!* epitomized its innovations and is considered the first production in U.S. history to be intentionally marketed as a fully integrated musical.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 1943, Rodgers became the ninth president of the [Dramatists Guild of America](/wiki/Dramatists_Guild_of_America "Dramatists Guild of America"). In November 1943, he and Hart mounted a revival of *[A Connecticut Yankee](/wiki/A_Connecticut_Yankee_(musical) "A Connecticut Yankee (musical)")*; Hart died from alcoholism and pneumonia just days after its opening.

Rodgers and Hammerstein wrote four more hits that are among the most popular in musical history. Each was made into a successful film: *[Carousel](/wiki/Carousel_(musical) "Carousel (musical)")* (1945), *[South Pacific](/wiki/South_Pacific_(musical) "South Pacific (musical)")* (1949, winner of the 1950 [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") for Drama), *[The King and I](/wiki/The_King_and_I "The King and I")* (1951), and *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music "The Sound of Music")* (1959). Other shows include the minor hit *[Flower Drum Song](/wiki/Flower_Drum_Song "Flower Drum Song")* (1958) and the relative failures *[Allegro](/wiki/Allegro_(musical) "Allegro (musical)")* (1947), *[Me and Juliet](/wiki/Me_and_Juliet "Me and Juliet")* (1953), and *[Pipe Dream](/wiki/Pipe_Dream_(musical) "Pipe Dream (musical)")* (1955). They also wrote the score to the film *[State Fair](/wiki/State_Fair_(1945_film) "State Fair (1945 film)")* (1945, remade in 1962 with [Pat Boone](/wiki/Pat_Boone "Pat Boone")) and a special TV musical of *[Cinderella](/wiki/Cinderella_(Rodgers_and_Hammerstein_musical) "Cinderella (Rodgers and Hammerstein musical)")* (1957).

Their collaboration produced many well-known songs, including "[Oh, What a Beautiful Mornin'](/wiki/Oh,_What_a_Beautiful_Mornin%27 "Oh, What a Beautiful Mornin'")", "[People Will Say We're in Love](/wiki/People_Will_Say_We%27re_in_Love "People Will Say We're in Love")", "[Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma_(Rodgers_and_Hammerstein_song) "Oklahoma (Rodgers and Hammerstein song)")" (which became Oklahoma's state song), "It's A Grand Night For Singing", "[If I Loved You](/wiki/If_I_Loved_You "If I Loved You")", "[You'll Never Walk Alone](/wiki/You%27ll_Never_Walk_Alone "You'll Never Walk Alone")", "[It Might as Well Be Spring](/wiki/It_Might_as_Well_Be_Spring "It Might as Well Be Spring")", "[Some Enchanted Evening](/wiki/Some_Enchanted_Evening "Some Enchanted Evening")", "[Younger Than Springtime](/wiki/Younger_Than_Springtime "Younger Than Springtime")", "[Bali Hai](/wiki/Bali_Hai "Bali Hai")", "[Getting to Know You](/wiki/Getting_to_Know_You_(song) "Getting to Know You (song)")", "[My Favorite Things](/wiki/My_Favorite_Things_(song) "My Favorite Things (song)")", "[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music_(song) "The Sound of Music (song)")", "[Sixteen Going on Seventeen](/wiki/Sixteen_Going_on_Seventeen "Sixteen Going on Seventeen")", "[Climb Ev'ry Mountain](/wiki/Climb_Ev%27ry_Mountain "Climb Ev'ry Mountain")", "[Do-Re-Mi](/wiki/Do-Re-Mi "Do-Re-Mi")", and "[Edelweiss](/wiki/Edelweiss_(song) "Edelweiss (song)")", Hammerstein's last song.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Richard_Rodgers_Ed_Sullivan_1952.JPG/250px-Richard_Rodgers_Ed_Sullivan_1952.JPG)](/wiki/File:Richard_Rodgers_Ed_Sullivan_1952.JPG)

Rodgers was the subject of a two-part special on [Ed Sullivan](/wiki/Ed_Sullivan "Ed Sullivan")'s *Toast of the Town* television show in 1952

Much of Rodgers's work with Hart and Hammerstein was orchestrated by [Robert Russell Bennett](/wiki/Robert_Russell_Bennett "Robert Russell Bennett"). Rodgers composed 12 themes that Bennett used in preparing the orchestra score for the 26-episode [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") television documentary *[Victory at Sea](/wiki/Victory_at_Sea "Victory at Sea")* (1952–53). This [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") production pioneered the "compilation documentary"—programming based on preexisting footage—and was broadcast in dozens of countries. The melody of the popular song "[No Other Love](/wiki/No_Other_Love_(1953_song) "No Other Love (1953 song)")" was later taken from the *Victory at Sea* theme "Beneath the Southern Cross". Rodgers won an [Emmy](/wiki/Emmy "Emmy") for the music for the ABC documentary *[Winston Churchill: The Valiant Years](/wiki/The_Valiant_Years "The Valiant Years")*, scored by [Eddie Sauter](/wiki/Eddie_Sauter "Eddie Sauter"), [Hershy Kay](/wiki/Hershy_Kay "Hershy Kay"), and [Robert Emmett Dolan](/wiki/Robert_Emmett_Dolan "Robert Emmett Dolan"). He composed the theme music, "[March of the Clowns](/w/index.php?title=March_of_the_Clowns&action=edit&redlink=1 "March of the Clowns (page does not exist)")", for the 1963–64 television series *[The Greatest Show on Earth](/wiki/The_Greatest_Show_on_Earth_(TV_series) "The Greatest Show on Earth (TV series)")*, which ran for 30 episodes, and contributed the main title theme for the 1963–64 historical anthology television series *[The Great Adventure](/wiki/The_Great_Adventure_(U.S._TV_series) "The Great Adventure (U.S. TV series)")*.

In 1950, [Rodgers and Hammerstein](/wiki/Rodgers_and_Hammerstein "Rodgers and Hammerstein") received [The Hundred Year Association of New York](/wiki/The_Hundred_Year_Association_of_New_York "The Hundred Year Association of New York")'s Gold Medal Award "in recognition of outstanding contributions to the City of New York." Rodgers, Hammerstein, and [Joshua Logan](/wiki/Joshua_Logan "Joshua Logan") won the [Pulitzer Prize for Drama](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Drama "Pulitzer Prize for Drama") for *South Pacific*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Rodgers and Hammerstein had won a [special Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_Special_Citations_and_Awards "Pulitzer Prize Special Citations and Awards") in 1944 for *Oklahoma!*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1954, Rodgers conducted the [New York Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/New_York_Philharmonic_Orchestra "New York Philharmonic Orchestra") in excerpts from *Victory at Sea*, *[Slaughter on Tenth Avenue](/wiki/Slaughter_on_Tenth_Avenue "Slaughter on Tenth Avenue")*, and the *[Carousel Waltz](/w/index.php?title=Carousel_Waltz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Carousel Waltz (page does not exist)")* for a special LP released by [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records").

[Rodgers and Hammerstein](/wiki/Rodgers_and_Hammerstein "Rodgers and Hammerstein") musicals earned a total of 37 [Tony Awards](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award"), 15 [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), two [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize"), two [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award"), and two [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award").

### After Hammerstein

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: After Hammerstein")]

Rodgers composed five more musicals after Hammerstein's death in 1960: *[No Strings](/wiki/No_Strings "No Strings")* (1962), *[Do I Hear a Waltz?](/wiki/Do_I_Hear_a_Waltz%3F "Do I Hear a Waltz?")* (1965), *[Two by Two](/wiki/Two_by_Two_(musical) "Two by Two (musical)")* (1970), *[Rex](/wiki/Rex_(musical) "Rex (musical)")* (1976), and *[I Remember Mama](/wiki/I_Remember_Mama_(musical) "I Remember Mama (musical)")* (1979).[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12)

Rodgers wrote both words and music for *No Strings*, which earned two Tony Awards and played 580 shows. The show was a minor hit and featured the song "[The Sweetest Sounds](/wiki/The_Sweetest_Sounds_(song) "The Sweetest Sounds (song)")".

Rodgers also wrote both the words and music for two new songs used in the film version of [*The Sound of Music*](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music_(film) "The Sound of Music (film)"). (Other songs in the film are by Rodgers and Hammerstein.)

Rodgers's final Broadway musicals had declining success as Rodgers was overshadowed by up-and-coming composers and lyricists. This was evident by the steady drop in run times and critic reviews. *Do I Hear a Waltz?* ran 220 performances; *Two by Two,* 343; *Rex*, only 49; and *I Remember Mama,* 108.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) Rodgers worked with lyricists [Stephen Sondheim](/wiki/Stephen_Sondheim "Stephen Sondheim") (*Do I Hear a Waltz?*), a protégé of Hammerstein; [Martin Charnin](/wiki/Martin_Charnin "Martin Charnin") (*Two by Two,* *I Remember Mama*); and [Sheldon Harnick](/wiki/Sheldon_Harnick "Sheldon Harnick") (*Rex*), but never found another permanent partner. Sondheim's reluctance to participate in *Do I Hear a Waltz?* led to tension between the two. Charnin and Rodgers had opposing ideas when creating *Two by Two*.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12)

At its 1978 commencement ceremonies, [Barnard College](/wiki/Barnard_College "Barnard College") awarded Rodgers its highest honor, the [Barnard Medal of Distinction](/wiki/List_of_Barnard_College_people#Recipients_of_the_Medal_of_Distinction "List of Barnard College people"). Also in 1978, Rodgers was an honoree at the first [Kennedy Center Honors](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honors "Kennedy Center Honors"). At the 1979 Tony Awards ceremony (six months before his death), he received the Lawrence Langner Memorial Award for Distinguished Lifetime Achievement in the American Theatre.

One of Rodgers's final works was a revival of *Fly With Me* for the 1980 Varsity Show, to which he added several new songs. He died less than four months before its premiere in April 1980.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

In 1930, Rodgers married [Dorothy Belle Feiner](/wiki/Dorothy_Rodgers "Dorothy Rodgers").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Their daughter, [Mary](/wiki/Mary_Rodgers "Mary Rodgers"), was the composer of *[Once Upon a Mattress](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Mattress "Once Upon a Mattress")* and an author of children's books.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The Rodgerses later lost a daughter at birth. Another daughter, Linda, also had a brief career as a songwriter. Mary's son [Adam Guettel](/wiki/Adam_Guettel "Adam Guettel"), also a musical theater composer, won Tony Awards for Best Score and Best Orchestrations for *[The Light in the Piazza](/wiki/The_Light_in_the_Piazza_(musical) "The Light in the Piazza (musical)")* in 2005. [Peter Melnick](/wiki/Peter_Melnick "Peter Melnick"), Linda Rodgers's son, is the composer of *Adrift In Macao*, which debuted at the Philadelphia Theatre Company in 2005 and was produced [Off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Broadway "Off-Broadway") in 2007. Mary Rodgers's book *Shy: The Alarmingly Outspoken Memoirs of Mary Rodgers* was published posthumously in 2022, and included frank revelations and assessments of her father, family, and herself.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Rodgers was an [atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist").[[16]](#cite_note-16) He was prone to depression and alcohol abuse, and was at one time hospitalized.

[Tom Drake](/wiki/Tom_Drake "Tom Drake") portrayed Rodgers in the [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer") film *[Words and Music](/wiki/Words_and_Music_(1948_film) "Words and Music (1948 film)")*, a semi-fictionalized depiction of the partnership of Rodgers and Hart. In [Richard Linklater](/wiki/Richard_Linklater "Richard Linklater")'s 2025 film *[Blue Moon](/wiki/Blue_Moon_(2025_film) "Blue Moon (2025 film)")*, he is played by [Andrew Scott](/wiki/Andrew_Scott_(actor) "Andrew Scott (actor)"), who won the [Silver Bear for Best Supporting Performance](/wiki/Silver_Bear_for_Best_Supporting_Performance "Silver Bear for Best Supporting Performance") at the [75th Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/75th_Berlin_International_Film_Festival "75th Berlin International Film Festival").

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Death")]

Rodgers died at his home in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") on December 30, 1979, at the age of 77,[[17]](#cite_note-17) after surviving cancer of the jaw, a heart attack, and a [laryngectomy](/wiki/Laryngectomy "Laryngectomy"). He was cremated, and his ashes were scattered at sea.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Legacy")]

In 1990, the 46th Street Theatre was renamed the [Richard Rodgers Theatre](/wiki/Richard_Rodgers_Theatre "Richard Rodgers Theatre") in his memory. In 1999, Rodgers and Hart were each commemorated on U.S. postage stamps. In 2002, the centennial year of Rodgers's birth was celebrated worldwide with books, retrospectives, performances, new recordings of his music, and a Broadway revival of *Oklahoma!*. The [BBC Proms](/wiki/BBC_Proms "BBC Proms") that year devoted an entire evening to Rodgers's music, including a concert performance of *Oklahoma!*, and the [Boston Pops Orchestra](/wiki/Boston_Pops_Orchestra "Boston Pops Orchestra") released the CD *My Favorite Things: A Richard Rodgers Celebration*.

[Alec Wilder](/wiki/Alec_Wilder "Alec Wilder") wrote:

> Of all the writers whose songs are considered and examined in this book, those of Rodgers show the highest degree of consistent excellence, inventiveness, and sophistication ... after spending weeks playing his songs, I am more than impressed and respectful: I am astonished.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In 2003, [PS 166](/wiki/PS_166 "PS 166") on [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")'s [Upper West Side](/wiki/Upper_West_Side "Upper West Side") was renamed The Richard Rodgers School of The Arts and Technology.

Rodgers is a member of the [American Theater Hall of Fame](/wiki/American_Theater_Hall_of_Fame "American Theater Hall of Fame").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Along with the Academy of Arts and Letters, Rodgers also started and endowed an award for non-established musical theater composers to produce new productions either by way of full productions or staged readings. It is the only award for which the Academy of Arts and Letters accepts applications and is presented every year. ### Relationship with performers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Rodgers&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Relationship with performers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Rodgers_and_Hammerstein_and_Berlin_and_Tamiris_NYWTS.jpg/250px-Rodgers_and_Hammerstein_and_Berlin_and_Tamiris_NYWTS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rodgers_and_Hammerstein_and_Berlin_and_Tamiris_NYWTS.jpg)

Richard Rodgers, [Irving Berlin](/wiki/Irving_Berlin "Irving Berlin"), [Oscar Hammerstein II](/wiki/Oscar_Hammerstein_II "Oscar Hammerstein II"), and [Helen Tamiris](/wiki/Helen_Tamiris "Helen Tamiris") watching hopefuls who are being auditioned on stage of the St. James Theatre.

[Rosemary Clooney](/wiki/Rosemary_Clooney "Rosemary Clooney") recorded a version of "[Falling in Love with Love](/wiki/Falling_in_Love_with_Love "Falling in Love with Love")" by Rodgers, using a swing style. After the recording session Richard Rodgers told her pointedly that it should be sung as a waltz.[[22]](#cite_note-22) After [Doris Day](/wiki/Doris_Day "Doris Day") recorded "[I Have Dreamed](/wiki/I_Have_Dreamed_(song) "I Have Dreamed (song)")" in 1961, he wrote to her and her arranger, Jim Harbert, that theirs was the most beautiful rendition of his song he had ever heard.

After [Peggy Lee](/wiki/Peggy_Lee "Peggy Lee") recorded her version of "[Lover](/wiki/Lover_(Rodgers_and_Hart_song) "Lover (Rodgers and Hart song)")", a Rodgers song, with a dramatically different arrangement from that originally conceived by him, Rodgers said, "I don't know why Peggy picked on me, she could have fucked up [Silent Night](/wiki/Silent_Night "Silent Night")".[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Mary Martin](/wiki/Mary_Martin "Mary Martin") said that Richard Rodgers composed songs for her for *South Pacific*, knowing she had a small vocal range, and the songs generally made her look her best. She also said that Rodgers and Hammerstein listened to all her suggestions and she worked extremely well with them.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Both Rodgers and Hammerstein wanted Doris Day for the lead in the film version of *South Pacific* and she reportedly wanted the part. They discussed it with her, but after her manager/husband [Martin Melcher](/wiki/Martin_Melcher "Martin Melcher") would not budge on his demand for a high salary for her, the role went to [Mitzi Gaynor](/wiki/Mitzi_Gaynor "Mitzi Gaynor").
